explanation_md: `v.begin()` points at 10. After one `++it`, it points at 20, which is printed. Two more increments move it past 30 to 40, which is printed. The result is `20` then `40`, each on its own line.

prompt_md: What does `v.end()` return for a non-empty `std::vector<int> v`?

options.a: An iterator pointing one past the last element — it must not be dereferenced.
options.b: An iterator pointing at the last element.
options.c: The integer index of the last element.
options.d: An iterator pointing at the first element, same as `v.begin()`.

explanation_md: `end()` is a past-the-end sentinel. It marks where the range stops and must never be dereferenced — doing so is undefined behaviour. To access the last element you would use `v.back()` or `*(v.end() - 1)` for random-access iterators.

options.a: `it *= 2` tries to multiply the iterator itself, not the element it points at; it should be `*it *= 2`.
options.b: The loop condition should be `it < v.end()` rather than `it != v.end()`.
options.c: `auto` cannot be used to declare an iterator; you must write `std::vector<int>::iterator`.
options.d: The second loop uses a separate iterator, so it will not see the modifications.

explanation_md: Iterators are not integers. `it *= 2` attempts to multiply the iterator object itself, which will not compile (or produce a nonsensical result for pointer-based iterators). The correct form is `*it *= 2` — dereference first to get a reference to the element, then multiply.

prompt_md: Why should you write `it != v.end()` rather than `it < v.end()` as the loop condition when using iterators?

options.a: `!=` works for all standard container iterators, while `<` only works for random-access iterators like those of `std::vector` and `std::array`.
options.b: `<` compares memory addresses, which causes undefined behaviour.
options.c: `!=` is faster than `<` for all container types.
options.d: `it < v.end()` will never be true, so the loop would never run.

explanation_md: Only random-access iterators (like those of `std::vector` and `std::array`) support `<`. Bidirectional iterators (like those of `std::list`) only support `==` and `!=`. Using `!=` makes your iterator loops portable across more container types.

The first loop adds 1 to each element through the iterator, turning `{4,5,6}` into `{5,6,7}`. The second loop prints each element followed by a space, then a newline, giving `5 6 7 ` with a trailing space.
